---
title: "Research Bridges: Reverse-Time SDEs, Probability-Flow ODEs, Flow Matching, and Control"
description: "How the ODE and dynamical-systems language of vector fields, flow maps, stability, and control reappears in modern generative modeling through reverse-time SDEs, probability-flow ODEs, and flow matching."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: odes-and-dynamical-systems
prerequisites:
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/concepts/discretization-time-stepping-and-the-bridge-to-control.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain why reverse-time SDEs, probability-flow ODEs, and flow matching reuse the same vector-field language as dynamical systems."
  - "Distinguish the stochastic reverse-time SDE view from the deterministic probability-flow ODE view."
  - "Recognize flow matching as learning a transport velocity field rather than reversing a noising process."
  - "Understand what the control analogy clarifies and where it should not be overextended."
applications:
  - machine-learning
  - control-and-dynamics
  - generative-modeling
keywords:
  - reverse-time SDE
  - probability-flow ODE
  - flow matching
  - control
  - vector field
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/concepts/discretization-time-stepping-and-the-bridge-to-control.qmd
  - /applications/machine-learning/score-matching-and-the-sde-view-of-diffusion.qmd
  - /applications/machine-learning/flow-matching-and-transport-views-of-generation.qmd
  - /applications/machine-learning/diffusion-models-and-denoising.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "Stanford CME296 Diffusion and Large Vision Models"
  - "Stanford EE363 Linear Dynamical Systems"
  - "Song et al. score-based SDE paper"
  - "Lipman et al. flow matching paper"
stable-source-dates:
  - "2026-04-25"
current-sources:
  - "Stanford CME296 bulletin"
  - "Stanford EE363 bulletin"
  - "Song et al. 2021 score-based SDE paper"
  - "Flow Matching for Generative Modeling"
  - "Stochastic Interpolants JMLR"
  - "Stanford ENGR209A bulletin"
---

## Role

This is an optional research bridge in the `ODEs and Dynamical Systems` module.

Its job is to show why modern ML papers keep reusing the same continuous-time language:

`state, vector field, trajectory, flow map, stability, control`

even when the application is generative modeling instead of a physical dynamical system.

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Discretization, Time-Stepping, and the Bridge to Control](discretization-time-stepping-and-the-bridge-to-control.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- why reverse-time SDEs and probability-flow ODEs are both continuous-time generative dynamics
- why flow matching is a vector-field-learning story
- why ODE and control language clarifies these models
- where the analogy to classical control is useful and where it is only partial

## Why It Matters

By the time you reach modern papers on diffusion, score models, flow matching, or continuous-depth learning, the notation often looks like this:

$$
dx_t = f(x_t,t)\,dt + g(t)\,dW_t,
\qquad
\frac{dx_t}{dt}=v(x_t,t),
\qquad
x_{k+1}=\Psi_h(x_k,u_k).
$$

At first glance, these may seem like separate worlds:

- stochastic diffusion
- deterministic transport
- numerical ODE integration
- sampled-data control

But from the ODE viewpoint, they are all about evolving state in time under a field.

This matters because it gives you a reusable reading strategy:

- identify the state
- identify the field or drift
- decide whether the evolution is stochastic or deterministic
- ask what quantity is preserved, dissipated, or controlled
- ask what discretization or solver actually produces samples

That is often the difference between being lost in notation and actually seeing the structure of a paper.

## Prerequisite Recall

- an ODE induces a flow map over time
- discretization turns a continuous flow into an iterated update rule
- Lyapunov-style reasoning studies what decreases or stays invariant along trajectories
- a controlled system adds an input that changes the state evolution rule
- the ML bridge pages already explained the basic stories of [Score Matching and the SDE View of Diffusion](../../../../applications/machine-learning/score-matching-and-the-sde-view-of-diffusion.qmd) and [Flow Matching and Transport Views of Generation](../../../../applications/machine-learning/flow-matching-and-transport-views-of-generation.qmd)

## Intuition

### Reverse-Time SDEs Are Stochastic Dynamics Guided By A Learned Field

In score-based diffusion, the forward process adds noise and turns data into something simpler.

The reverse process is then described by a reverse-time SDE whose drift uses a learned score field.

So the model is not memorizing outputs directly. It is learning a field that guides stochastic state evolution backward toward data.

### Probability-Flow ODEs Replace Stochastic Reverse Motion With Deterministic Flow

The same score model also induces a deterministic ODE with the same one-time marginals as the reverse SDE.

That is the probability-flow ODE viewpoint:

`replace stochastic reverse dynamics by a deterministic continuous flow that transports the same distributions`

This makes the generative story look even more like classical ODE evolution.

### Flow Matching Learns Velocity Fields Directly

Flow matching goes one step further in ODE language.

Instead of starting from a diffusion and then deriving a reverse dynamic, it directly learns a time-dependent velocity field that transports source samples to data.

So the organizing object is not a denoising rule but a transport vector field.

### Control Is The Right Analogy, But Only Up To A Point

The control lens helps because many of the questions are familiar:

- what is the state
- what law moves it
- what inputs or fields are applied over time
- what discretization is actually implemented
- how do errors accumulate under repeated stepping

But generative modeling is not classical feedback control in disguise.

Usually:

- there is no external controller stabilizing an observed plant in real time
- the learned field is part of the model itself
- the objective is distribution transport or sampling quality, not regulation to a reference trajectory

So the right mindset is:

`control provides mathematical language and intuition, not a literal one-to-one identification`

## Formal Core

::: {#def-reverse-sde}
## Definition: Reverse-Time SDE View

A reverse-time SDE is a stochastic state evolution rule run backward in time, typically written in the form

$$
dx = b(x,t)\,dt + \sigma(t)\,d\bar W_t,
$$

where the drift `b` depends on a learned score field.
:::

The essential point is that the generative process is still a dynamical law on state space, but now with stochastic forcing.

::: {#def-probflow}
## Definition: Probability-Flow ODE

A probability-flow ODE is a deterministic ODE whose time marginals match those of a corresponding diffusion model:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt}=v(x,t).
$$
:::

This gives a transport-style description of the same distribution evolution.

::: {#def-flow-matching}
## Definition: Flow Matching

Flow matching learns a time-dependent velocity field `v_\theta(x,t)` so that integrating

$$
\frac{dx}{dt}=v_\theta(x,t)
$$

transports a simple source distribution toward the data distribution along a chosen probability path.
:::

::: {#thm-bridge}
## Theorem Idea: Same Distributions, Different Dynamic Realizations

In the score-based setting, the reverse-time SDE and the probability-flow ODE can induce the same time-dependent probability marginals even though one evolution is stochastic and the other is deterministic.
:::

At first pass, the lesson is:

- one mathematical object describes sample paths
- another describes the evolution of distributions
- a paper may switch between these viewpoints depending on what it wants to prove or compute

::: {#thm-control-lens}
## Theorem Idea: Discretization Turns Learned Continuous Dynamics Into Update Rules

Whether the continuous-time model is an SDE or an ODE, actual sampling or control implementation eventually uses a discrete solver or update rule.
:::

So the ODE module's discretization page is not side context. It is part of the real computational story.

## A Small Worked Example

Here is a deliberately stripped-down comparison table for one state variable `x_t`.

### Reverse-Time SDE Lens

We write

$$
dx_t = b(x_t,t)\,dt + \sigma(t)\,d\bar W_t.
$$

Interpretation:

- `b(x_t,t)` is the learned drift pushing samples toward data-like regions
- `\sigma(t)\,d\bar W_t` keeps the evolution stochastic
- sampling requires simulating noisy dynamics backward in time

### Probability-Flow ODE Lens

We write

$$
\frac{dx_t}{dt}=v(x_t,t).
$$

Interpretation:

- the randomness is moved to the initial condition
- the subsequent evolution is deterministic
- sampling becomes ODE integration

### Flow-Matching Lens

We again write

$$
\frac{dx_t}{dt}=v_\theta(x_t,t),
$$

but now the field is learned directly from a transport objective rather than derived from reversing a diffusion.

Interpretation:

- choose a probability path from source to data
- supervise the velocity field along that path
- generate by solving the learned ODE

### Control Lens

A classical sampled control update looks like

$$
x_{k+1}=\Psi_h(x_k,u_k).
$$

The analogy is:

- the learned field plays a role similar to a time-dependent law shaping the evolution
- the solver turns continuous dynamics into a discrete update chain

The non-analogy is:

- in generative modeling, the goal is usually to transport distributions, not regulate a plant around an operating point

## Computation Lens

The most useful computational question is often not

`is this paper using ODEs or SDEs?`

but rather

`what object is actually being integrated, and what discretization error matters?`

For example:

- reverse-time SDE samplers involve stochastic integration choices
- probability-flow ODE samplers involve deterministic solver choices
- flow-matching models often emphasize trajectory geometry because straighter paths can reduce solver cost

So the solver is part of the model story, not only an implementation detail.

## Application Lens

### Diffusion And Score-Based Generative Modeling

The reverse-time SDE and probability-flow ODE views explain why diffusion papers talk about drift, noise schedules, trajectories, and solvers.

### Flow Matching And Transport

Flow matching makes the ODE viewpoint explicit: learn a velocity field and integrate it.

### Control And Dynamical-Systems Thinking

Control contributes the language of state evolution, held inputs, stability intuition, and discretization-aware implementation, even when the application target is not a physical plant.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- why reverse-time SDEs and probability-flow ODEs are two continuous-time views of generation
- why flow matching is a learned vector-field story
- why discretization remains central in all of them
- why the control analogy is helpful but partial

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

After this page, the strongest adjacent pages are:

- [ODEs and Dynamical Systems](../index.qmd)
- [Discretization, Time-Stepping, and the Bridge to Control](discretization-time-stepping-and-the-bridge-to-control.qmd)
- [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](../../numerical-methods/concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)
- [Score Matching and the SDE View of Diffusion](../../../../applications/machine-learning/score-matching-and-the-sde-view-of-diffusion.qmd)
- [Flow Matching and Transport Views of Generation](../../../../applications/machine-learning/flow-matching-and-transport-views-of-generation.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this page are:

- [Stanford CME296 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2276231) - official Stanford course listing that explicitly groups diffusion, score matching, and flow matching in one modern generative curriculum. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.13456) - primary source for the reverse-time SDE and probability-flow ODE viewpoints. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Flow Matching for Generative Modeling](https://openreview.net/forum?id=PqvMRDCJT9t) - primary source for learning transport vector fields directly. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stochastic Interpolants: A Unifying Framework for Flows and Diffusions](https://www.jmlr.org/papers/v26/23-1605.html) - current JMLR source that unifies transport and diffusion-style paths in one framework. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE363 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2273941) - official linear-dynamical-systems course description linking state evolution, control, and estimation. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford ENGR209A bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1043471) - official nonlinear-systems course description that keeps the stability and control lens visible. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [Stanford CME296 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2276231) - `First pass` - official Stanford course listing that places diffusion, score matching, and flow matching in one continuous-time ML arc. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.13456) - `First pass` - primary source for reverse-time SDEs and the probability-flow ODE. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Flow Matching for Generative Modeling](https://openreview.net/forum?id=PqvMRDCJT9t) - `First pass` - primary source for the direct vector-field-learning viewpoint. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stochastic Interpolants: A Unifying Framework for Flows and Diffusions](https://www.jmlr.org/papers/v26/23-1605.html) - `Second pass` - current unifying framework for deterministic and stochastic bridges between distributions. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE363 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2273941) - `Second pass` - official course description linking state evolution, control, estimation, and discretization-aware thinking. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford ENGR209A bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1043471) - `Second pass` - official nonlinear-systems course description that keeps Lyapunov and control language in view. Checked `2026-04-25`.
